user_prompt: A user requested deletion of all their data. Generate the DSAR-deletion checklist including which systems to scrub.
actual_output:
## DSAR Erasure Request — Article 17 Checklist

*Compliance guidance only — adapt to your specific stack and consult counsel for edge cases.*

### Step 1: Verify and Log the Request (Day 1-2)

- [ ] **Authenticate the requester** — confirm identity via account-linked email/credentials (avoid requesting excessive ID per Art. 12(6))
- [ ] **Log the request** in your DSAR register with: date received, requester ID, scope, deadline (30 days from receipt, extendable to 90 with justification — Art. 12(3))
- [ ] **Acknowledge receipt** to the requester within 72 hours (best practice)
- [ ] **Determine scope** — full erasure or specific data categories?

### Step 2: Assess Grounds and Exemptions — Art. 17(1) & 17(3)

Confirm at least one Art. 17(1) ground applies (typically: withdrawal of consent, data no longer necessary, or objection under Art. 21).

**Check for exemptions before deleting** — you may need to retain data for:
- [ ] Legal obligations (tax records, accounting — typically 6-10 years)
- [ ] Establishment/exercise/defense of legal claims
- [ ] Freedom of expression and information
- [ ] Public interest, archiving, scientific/historical research

**Action:** Document which data must be retained under exemption and the legal basis. The user must be informed if you partially refuse.

### Step 3: Systems to Scrub — Standard SaaS Inventory

#### Primary Systems
- [ ] **Production database** — user record, profile data, linked records (orders, posts, comments)
- [ ] **Authentication system** (Auth0, Cognito, Firebase Auth, internal) — credentials, MFA tokens, session records
- [ ] **Application logs** — anonymize or delete entries containing personal identifiers
- [ ] **Audit logs** — assess retention obligation; if required for security, pseudonymize where possible

#### Backups and Archives
- [ ] **Database backups** — document policy: most controllers flag the user as "to be erased on restore" rather than rewriting backups (acceptable per EDPB guidance if backup is isolated and erasure occurs on restore)
- [ ] **Cold storage / S3 archives / Glacier** — locate and delete
- [ ] **Data warehouse** (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift) — production tables and historical snapshots

#### Communication and Marketing
- [ ] **Email service provider** (Mailchimp, SendGrid, Customer.io) — delete contact and suppression list handling (note: keep minimal hashed record on suppression list to honor opt-out)
- [ ] **Transactional email logs** — purge per retention policy
- [ ] **CRM** (HubSpot, Salesforce, Pipedrive) — contact, activity, notes
- [ ] **Customer support** (Zendesk, Intercom, Freshdesk) — tickets, chat transcripts, attachments

#### Analytics and Tracking
- [ ] **Product analytics** (Mixpanel, Amplitude, Heap, PostHog) — use their GDPR deletion APIs
- [ ] **Web analytics** (GA4, Matomo) — submit user deletion request via API/UI
- [ ] **Session replay** (FullStory, Hotjar, LogRocket) — delete recordings
- [ ] **Error monitoring** (Sentry, Bugsnag, Rollbar) — scrub user context from events

#### Infrastructure and Operations
- [ ] **CDN logs** — purge access logs containing IPs/identifiers
- [ ] **Web server logs** — anonymize IP addresses
- [ ] **Feature flag tools** (LaunchDarkly, Split) — remove user targeting records
- [ ] **A/B testing platforms** — remove from experiment cohorts

#### Internal Tools
- [ ] **Data exports / BI dashboards** — check for cached personal data
- [ ] **Slack / Teams** — search for and remove personal data shared in channels (best effort)
- [ ] **Shared drives** (Google Drive, SharePoint) — search and remove documents containing the user's data
- [ ] **Spreadsheets and ad-hoc exports** — frequent compliance gap

### Step 4: Notify Processors and Third Parties — Art. 17(2) & Art. 19

- [ ] **Notify all sub-processors** with active copies of the data — your DPA should require their cooperation
- [ ] **Notify any controllers you've shared data with** under Art. 19, unless impossible or disproportionate effort
- [ ] If data was made public, take "reasonable steps" to inform other controllers processing it

### Step 5: Confirm and Document (By Day 30)

- [ ] **Run verification query** across systems to confirm no residual personal data
- [ ] **Document erasure** in DSAR register: systems scrubbed, retained data + lawful basis, completion date
- [ ] **Respond to requester** in writing, confirming:
  - What was deleted
  - What was retained and why (if applicable)
  - Right to lodge complaint with supervisory authority
  - Right to judicial remedy
- [ ] **Retain the DSAR record itself** (the request and your response) as evidence of compliance — this is a legitimate retention purpose

### Common Gaps to Watch

⚠️ **Backups left undocumented** — have a written backup-erasure policy
⚠️ **Forgotten SaaS tools** — maintain a current processor inventory
⚠️ **Cached/derived data** — ML training sets, aggregated reports retaining identifiers
⚠️ **Employee inboxes** — emails containing the user's data; consider retention policy
⚠️ **Suppression lists** — keep hashed identifier only, not full PII

### Suggested Response Template (excerpt)

> Dear [Name],
>
> We confirm that, in response to your erasure request received on [date] under Article 17 GDPR, we have deleted your
judge_reasoning: The chatbot provided a comprehensive, structured, and highly detailed checklist that directly addressed the user's request for a DSAR-deletion process and system inventory.
